coredata.dc:title: Facile and green synthesis of chlorophyll-derived multi-color fluorescent carbonized polymer dots and their use for sensitive detection of hemin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Tianjiao
Dong, Deming
Meng, Yingyi
Chen, Haijun
Liu, Chunyue
Qi, Zihan
Li, Anfeng
Ning, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Due to the very important role in physiological process, a simple and sensitive hemin detection method is necessarily required. Biomass-based carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have been widely studied especially as fluorescence probe owing to the advantages of low toxicity and the variety of fluorescence color, yet there are still challenges in developing their multi-color emission property from the same raw materials. In this work, red, white and blue emissive CPDs derived from chlorophyll have been synthesized via hydrothermal method. Then white-emitted CPDs (white-CPDs) with the Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates at (0.34, 0.32) were used to develop a fluorescence quenched sensing system for hemin determination. There is a good linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of hemin in the range of 0.1–0.95 μM with a detection limit of 0.043 μM, and the quenching mechanism was considered to be caused by inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, it has been successfully used for hemin detection in serum and also for visual determination, which indicating great potential in applications of disease diagnoses and trace identification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chlorophyll
Carbonized polymer dots
White-emission
Hemin
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment of fluorescence detection system for hemin based on white-CPDs
Investigation of quenching mechanism by hemin

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21404047).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the reaction process.

The fluorescence spectra of red-CPDs (A) white-CPDs (B) and blue-CPDs (C), with their photographs under visible (left) and UV (right) light (365 nm) inset; D) Absorption spectra of red/white/blue-CPDs; Fluorescence spectra with the normalized intensity (E) of the three kinds of CPDs under optimal excitation, and their corresponding CIE coordinates image (F).
TEM images of red-CPDs (A) white-CPDs (B) and blue-CPDs (C) with their high-resolution TEM images inset. Size distribution histograms of red-CPDs (D) white-CPDs (E) and blue-CPDs (F).
The XRD spectra (A) and FT-IR spectra (B) of red/white/blue-CPDs.

The high resolution C 1s XPS (A), N 1s XPS (B) and O 1s XPS (C) spectra of red-CPDs, respectively. The high resolution C 1s XPS (D), N 1s XPS (E) and O 1s XPS (F) spectra of white-CPDs, respectively. The high resolution C 1s XPS (G), N 1s XPS (H) and O 1s XPS (I) spectra of blue-CPDs, respectively. The high resolution C 1s XPS (J), N 1s XPS (K) and O 1s XPS (L) spectra of chlorophyll.

A) Fluorescence spectra of white-CPDs in the presence of hemin with different concentrations after incubated for 5 min; B) The linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and concentrations of hemin, the error bars were calculated through three parallel experiments. Inset: the corresponding fluorescence digital photos.

Effect of different substances (50 μM) on the fluorescence of white-CPDs (red bars), and the fluorescence response with the coexistence of above interfering components (50 μM) and hemin (0.5 μM) (blue bars).
A) UV-vis absorption spectrum of hemin and the excitation and emission spectra of white-CPDs; B) Fluorescence decay curves of white-CPDs with hemin in different concentration.
Energy levels of the LUMO and HOMO of white-CPDs and hemin.
Schematic plot of coffee or blood paper strips’ preparation, as well as the photographs of white-CPDs before and after the addition of paper strips with coffee or blood on.
Comparison of various methods for hemin detection
Detection of hemin in calf serum samples by white-CPDs.
Detection of hemin in calf serum samples by liquid chromatography.

head.ce:title: Facile and green synthesis of chlorophyll-derived multi-color fluorescent carbonized polymer dots and their use for sensitive detection of hemin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment Ministry of Education, College of New Energy and Environment, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Avenue, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment Ministry of Education
College of New Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Groundwater Resources and Environment Ministry of Education, College of New Energy and Environment, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Avenue, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the very important role in physiological process, a simple and sensitive hemin detection method is necessarily required. Biomass-based carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have been widely studied especially as fluorescence probe owing to the advantages of low toxicity and the variety of fluorescence color, yet there are still challenges in developing their multi-color emission property from the same raw materials. In this work, red, white and blue emissive CPDs derived from chlorophyll have been synthesized via hydrothermal method. Then white-emitted CPDs (white-CPDs) with the Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates at (0.34, 0.32) were used to develop a fluorescence quenched sensing system for hemin determination. There is a good linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of hemin in the range of 0.1–0.95 μM with a detection limit of 0.043 μM, and the quenching mechanism was considered to be caused by inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, it has been successfully used for hemin detection in serum and also for visual determination, which indicating great potential in applications of disease diagnoses and trace identification.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemin (iron protoporphyrin ⅠⅩ) is a kind of natural cyclic tetrapyrrole with iron contained. It is involved in the physiological processes including oxygen transport, electron transfer, drug metabolism and so on [1,2]. However, hemin is also the major source of iron for some bacterial pathogens [3], and the excess free hemin can wreak oxidative havoc, leading to inflammation, renal impairment, vascular disease and other damages [4]. Therefore, the hemin is considered to be harmful in these cases, and to develop a methodology for its detection is meaningful. So far, some methods such as liquid chromatography [5], chemiluminescence [6], electrochemical method [7] and fluorescence analysis [8] have been developed for the quantitative detection of hemin. Therein, fluorescence analysis is the outstanding one for its simple operation and equipment.

Among nanomaterials, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) as a classification of carbon dots (CDs) have promising applications in biological and chemical species detection for its tunable fluorescence properties [9]. While biomass refers to substances obtained directly from natural plants or animals, or components extracted from natural sources. For the advantages of low-cost, abundance and easily available, varieties of biomass such as crawfish shells [10], cranberry [11], Gynostemma [12], endosperm of Borassus flabellifer [13], rice residue [14], water hyacinth leaves [15], etc. have been selected as carbon sources to prepare CPDs, and many were through the hydrothermal method [16–18]. The biomass-based CPDs process lower toxicity and better biocompatibility [19–20], however, most of them exhibit a single emission of blue or green fluorescence [21], which is not conducive to the application in stable and reproducible warm light-emitting diode (LED), high-quality biological imaging and credible visual detection [22–26]. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the synthesis of multicolor emission biomass-based CPDs from the same raw materials.

Chlorophyll is a kind of magnesium porphyrin compound with near-infrared emission property, which widely presents in photosynthetic organisms. Due to its photosensitive, acid-sensitive, heat-sensitive and hydrophobic, chlorophyll cannot be directly used as fluorescence probe. However, the unique emission characteristics give chlorophyll excellent potential in synthesis of multi-emission CPDs, which have been extensively studied in the field of sensors. As recently reported, a number of CPDs have been prepared from chlorophyll-rich materials and applied on determination of analytes [24,27–31], though most were obtained through solvothermal methods. Hence, chlorophyll could be a proper precursor for the preparation of multicolor fluorescent CPDs.

Herein, based on the unique long-wavelength emission property of chlorophyll, we prepared three chlorophyll-derived CPDs through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, with the fluorescence emission of red, white and blue, respectively. The characterizations substantiate the as-prepared CPDs had the typical structure of carbonized core and polymer chains, and the cross-linking enhanced emission effect plays an important role in their fluorescence mechanism. Of which, the white-emitted CPDs showed triple emission property under 395 nm excitation with the CIE at (0.34, 0.32). Moreover, as a fluorescent probe, the white-emitted CPDs could achieve unique and sensitive hemin detection on the basis of inner filter effect, and had the potential to be used for other detection of porphyrin-ring structure molecules. This system could be used for quantitative detection of hemin in serum as well as for the fluorescence determination of blood based on naked eyes.

Fresh Spinach was obtained from local market. Anhydrous ethylenediamine (EDA) and Calf serum were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Reagents such as hemin, uridine, adenosine, sodium copper chlorophyll, vitamin B12, hemoglobin, cytochrome C and catalase were obtained from Aladdin (China). Chromatographic grade methyl alcohol and acetonitrile were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Shanghai, China). Acetone, glacial acetic acid, specpure potassium bromide (KBr) and other chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All these reagents were analytic reagent (AR) grade unless otherwise indicated and the distilled deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with a RF-6000 spectrophotometer from Shimadzu. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2600 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence lifetimes were measured with an FLS920 spectrometer from Edinburgh. The morphology and size of the samples were observed by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 200 kV by using super-thin carbon films as grids. The Fourier transfer infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analysis was carried out using a Shimadzu IR Tracer-100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by a Kratos AXIS SUPRA+ spectrometer from Shimadzu. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with an Empyrean X-ray diffractometer from PANalytical B.V. with the step size of 0.026. Shimadzu LC20AB high performance liquid chromatograph with C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm) was used for the measurement of hemin concentration in serum as a comparison. The electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI660E electrochemical station (CH Instruments, China).

First, chlorophyll as a precursor was prepared. Fresh spinach leaves were washed and dried at a ventilated place, then cut into filaments less than 2 mm. The filaments (25 g) were immersed in 100 mL acetone with a little calcium carbonate added and then performed ultrasound for 30 min. The mixture was filtered with glass fiber filter membranes (0.45 μm pore size), and the moist precipitate of chlorophyll was obtained after rotary evaporating at 50 °C for 25 min. After that, the chlorophyll precipitate was freeze-dried to obtain chlorophyll solid.

On this basis, chlorophyll solid (5 mg) was dissolved in an EDA aqueous solution (50 μL EDA in 20 mL H2O) through ultrasonic treatment. Then the above solution (8 mL) and H2O (2 mL) were transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL), where red-CPDs were obtained with 3 h reaction at 130 °C, white-CPDs were obtained with 10 h reaction at 130 °C and blue-CPDs were obtained with 10 h reaction at 200 °C. After filtering with polyethersulfone filter membranes (0.45 μm pore size), all these samples were conserved in dark at room temperature for the following experiments without further modification.

Hemin solution at a concentration of 99 mg/L was first prepared by dissolving hemin powder in 1% Na2CO3 (mass fraction) solution. Then, a certain amount hemin solution and white-CPDs solution were mixed to obtain the final sample with hemin concentration of 0.1-0.95 μM, and the shaken mixture was allowed to interact for 5 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra under the excitation of 395 nm were recorded. The preparation of white-CPDs, dispensing of hemin solution and operation of fluorescence titration were all performed for three times under the same programs, in order to evaluate the sensitivity and precision of the white-CPDs fluorescent nanoprobe for hemin detection.

In addition, some metal ions (Na+, K+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+ and Li+, 50 μM) and biomolecules (uridine, adenosine, L-arginine and glucose, 50 μM) were selected as potential interfering species that could cause fluorescence quenching of white-CPDs for evaluating the stability and selectivity of hemin determination by white-CPDs.

Calf serum for liquid chromatography detection was pretreated by adding twice the serum volume of acetonitrile, and the supernatant was obtained after vortex and centrifugation for further use. When white-CPDs were used for hemin detection, the serum was diluted 50-fold with 10 mM pH=7 Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution for back-up use [32].

Then the UV-vis absorption spectra of these CPDs were also measured. As shown in Fig.2D, the spectrum of white-CPDs had broad absorption and exhibited four significant characteristic peaks. Combining absorption spectra of the other five samples shown in Fig.2D and Fig.S1D (red/blue-CPDs, the hydrothermal products from pure EDA or chlorophyll and the chlorophyll solution) for comparative analysis, the peaks at 265 and 316 nm could be attributed to π-π* transition of C=C bonds and n-π* transition of C=O bonds in carbon cores formed by cross-linking polymerization and carbonization [24,36,37], while the peaks at 412 and 660 nm should be ascribed to Soret band and one of the Q-band in chlorophyll-derived porphyrin structure [28,38,39]. Besides, only the absorption peaks due to porphyrin structure could be clearly observed in the spectrum of red-CPDs, so it was presumed that part of porphyrin groups derived from chlorophyll were preserved. However, blue-CPDs only showed the absorption at short wavelength, demonstrating a deeper degree of cross-linking polymerization and carbonization under a higher temperature. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of red/white/blue-CPDs at their optimum emission wavelengths were monitored. Each samples contained a short-lived component τ1 (about 3.7 ns, attributed to the core states) and a long-lived component τ2 (about 9.1 ns, assigned to the surface states) [39,40]. From red-CPDs to blue-CPDs, average lifetimes and the proportion of τ2 were estimated to be 4.71, 5.02, 6.00 ns and 8.59, 10.2, 59.44%, respectively. It indicated that the influence of surface states in the fluorescence lifetimes of CPDs was increased with the deepening of the crosslinking polymerization and carbonization process.

To further investigate the probable formation and fluorescence mechanism, the hydrothermal products of chlorophyll, EDA at 130 °C 10 h, as well as chlorophyll solution dissolved in ethanol were prepared. As shown in Fig.S1A-C, products from pure EDA hydrothermal progress possessed obvious short-wavelength emission, chlorophyll solution exhibited strong characteristic emission at around 680 nm, while products from pure chlorophyll hydrothermal progress possessed two fluorescence emissions corresponding to those mentioned above. Combining these with the earlier results, it could be preliminarily speculated that chlorophyll underwent two processes as follow. On one hand, chlorophyll had been broken during the hydrothermal process and resulting in formation of fluorophores with blue emission. On the other hand, some of the inherent red-emission porphyrin units were not destroyed, so part of red emission was retained in products. Whereas EDA with two amino units mainly played a role of cross-linking polymerization in the preparation process of CPDs, with a polymer network and small molecule blue-emission fluorophores generated. Based on the CEE effect presented in all samples, the crosslinking during the reaction formed a stable and compact environment for the internal fluorophores [16,41], which contributed to the blue emission of CPDs while retained the red emission from chlorophyll. As the reaction progressed, the protective of polymer structure on chlorophyll-derived red-emission fluorophores was weakened accompanied with those fluorophores gradually destroyed, while the enhancement of blue fluorescence by CEE mechanism and surface states were highlighted. Eventually, the chlorophyll-derived CPDs showed fluorescence from red to blue (Fig.1).

Observed by TEM, the size and morphologies of prepared CPDs were investigated. As shown in Fig.3 A-C, all the CPDs had a spherical shape and evenly dispersed. The size distribution histograms (Fig.3D-F) were constructed by counting 100 particles, and the average diameter of the red/white/blue-CPDs was calculated to be 2.0±0.6 nm, 3.8±0.5 nm and 4.1±0.8 nm, respectively. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CPDs (inset of Fig.3A-C) all displayed similar well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm which could attributed to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [40,42]. The XRD patterns of the three samples (Fig.4 A) all exhibited an obvious broad diffraction peak at around 2θ=28.5° (pale yellow region), which probably derived from the limited ordering structure of cross-linked polymer skeleton [43]. These results also indicated a lower graphitization degree and an abundance existence of disordered polymer chains in CPDs. Therefore, it could be further confirmed that the preparation of CPDs underwent the process of polymerization, cross-linking and mild carbonization with carbon cores formed.

Additionally, the FT-IR spectra were recorded to characterize the surface compositions of obtained CPDs. As shown in Fig.4B, these three kinds of CPDs possessed similar chemical bonds, such as O-H and N-H at 3423 cm-1, =C-H and -C-H at 3190, 3016, 2924 cm-1, and the stretching vibrations of C-O at 1033 cm-1 [37,44,45]. Focusing on the characteristic peaks in the pale green region, it could be seen that from red-CPDs to blue-CPDs, the relative intensity of C=O at around 1593-1631 cm-1 [39,46] enhanced. This indicated the formation of more amide groups or carboxyl groups, which resulting in a higher crosslinking degree and hydrophilicity of the samples. The stretching vibration intensity of C=C at 1508 cm-1 [40] showed no significant change. While the peak of C-N at 1350 cm-1 [47] exhibited a slight increase, which was consistent with the speculation about the increase of amide groups.

The above analysis could be further verified through XPS. The full-scan XPS spectra of chlorophyll and the as-prepared CPDs (Fig.S2) showed the existence of three characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s appeared at 285.1, 399.8 and 531.4 eV, respectively. Therein the higher N content of the three kinds of CPDs was ascribed to the addition of EDA during the preparation process. Then the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O1s spectra of the four samples were analyzed. The C 1s spectra (Fig.5 A, D, G and J) all revealed the presence of C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.3 eV) and C=O (288.5 eV), however the spectra of these three kinds of CPDs exhibited an additional type of C at 292.5 eV which could be attributed to π-π* satellite [48]. As π-π* shake-up satellite was commonly regarded as a characteristic of aromatic or conjugated systems [49], the presence of this peak indicated a slight graphitization of CPDs. Besides, the increasing of C=O in samples from red-CPDs to blue-CPDs was in agreement with the results of FT-IR. The N 1s spectra (Fig.5B, E, H and K) was deconvoluted into pyridinic N at 398.0 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.5 eV and graphitic N at 401.1 eV [22]. According to the N 1s spectra of chlorophyll (Fig.5K), the existence of pyridinic N was related to the intrinsic structure of chlorophyll to a certain extent. The decrease of pyridinic N from red-CPDs to blue-CPDs and the emergence of graphitic N in CPDs were conformed to the foregoing speculation about these chlorophyll-derived CPDs’ reaction process. Moreover, the O 1s spectra showed two peaks of C=O and C-O, and the enhancement of the two chemical bonds through hydrothermal reaction was revealed (Fig.5C, F, I and L). To sum up, the structural analysis identified mild carbonization of the CPDs as well as the occurrence of cross-linked polymerization process, and the speculation that the fluorescence mechanism was mainly influenced by CEE and surface states was also further verified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescence detection system for hemin based on white-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: White light is a kind of compound light. Therefore, white-CPDs with multiple emission can easily achieve the transformation of its fluorescence color by adjusting the proportion of each emission peaks, and have the potential to be used in visualization detection. In order to investigate the usability of white-CPDs as fluorescence probe, the stability of white-CPDs was firstly explored. As shown in Fig.S3A, after two months’ preservation in dark, the aqueous solution of white-CPDs exhibited homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation, and the fluorescence intensity at 658 nm remained 76% with CIE coordinates located at (0.29, 0.31), which was still in the region of white light. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of white-CPDs in the presence of high concentration ionic (201 mM NaCl, 7.26 mM Na2CO3), oxidizing substance (890 mM H2O2) and organic solvent (1460 mM ethanol) could be maintained above 70% (Fig.S3B).

Afterwards, target substances with different structures were selected (at a concentration of 233 μM) to investigate the stability of white-CPDs, such as EDTA-2Na with structure of complexation, propenoic acid with structure of linear conjugated, tetrahydrofuran and dioxane with structure of cyclic unconjugated, 1,5 naphthalenediamine and pyrrole with structure of cyclic conjugated. As shown in Fig.S4A, all of them only caused minor changes in the fluorescence intensity of white-CPDs compared with the original CPDs. Moreover, according to the speculated reaction mechanism, the porphyrin units played an important role in the fluorescence color of as-prepared chlorophyll-derived CPDs, so sodium copper chlorophyll, vitamin B12, hemin as porphyrin-derived molecules were selected to verify their quenching effects on white-CPDs. It could be seen that white-CPDs exhibited three characteristic fluorescence peaks at 488, 603 and 658 nm, respectively, under an excitation of 395 nm (Fig.2E). With the concentration of sodium copper chlorophyll and vitamin B12 increased, the intensity of all three peaks decreased significantly and progressively (Fig.S4B-C). While the addition of hemin mainly quenched the emission at 658 nm, showing a proportional change of white-CPDs’ fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6 A). These results revealed that porphyrin structure could bring changes to the fluorescence intensity of white-CPDs, wherein the special proportional fluorescence quenching phenomenon causing by hemin in particular gave it potential to achieve visualization detection. Therefore, hemin was chosen as a proper target material for the establishment of fluorescence detection system based on white-CPDs.

On this basis, in order to investigate the fluorescence intensity effects of white-CPDs by other molecules with porphyrin-ring structures in body, cytochrome C, hemoglobin and catalase were chosen for further research. As shown in Fig.S5, with the concentration of testing molecules increased, the intensity of peak at 658 nm all decreased significantly and progressively, but there were some variances of the decrease fluorescence ratios between blue and red with different biomolecules. Combine the results shown in Fig.S4, it could be credibly described that the fluorescence detection system could be used for the quantitative detection of molecules with porphyrin characteristic structure. Due to their different chemical structure and molecular weight, the detection ranges were also different. Considering that these molecules were generally distributed in different parts and cells of the organism, this system might detect total amount or specific part quantity of porphyrin-ring structures according to the purpose and scenario of the test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of quenching mechanism by hemin
ce:section.ce:para: Generally speaking, the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching encompass static quenching, dynamic quenching, photo-induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). To explore the quenching mechanism by hemin, UV-vis spectra, FT-IR spectra and fluorescence decay curves of white-CPDs before and after the adjunction of hemin were tested firstly. In Fig.S6, it was evident that the intensity and location of the characteristic peaks in both UV-vis and IR spectra were almost unchanged, which indicated that there was no new substance generated during the process. Thereby, the possibility of static quenching was excluded. Meanwhile, Fig.8 B showed that the calculated average fluorescence lifetimes of white-CPDs in the presence of 0-2.0 μM hemin did not change significantly (from 5.03 to 4.64 ns), which meant that the quenching mechanism might not be from dynamic quenching or FRET, for the two usually leading an obvious reduction in the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescence probe [50]. Then, some optical properties of white-CPDs and hemin were investigated. As could be seen from Fig.8A, the ultraviolet absorption band of hemin had an obviously overlap with the excitation and emission band of white-CPDs, which suggested that the quenching mechanism might be originating from PET or IFE [27,51,52].

In order to determine whether the quenching mechanism belongs to PET or IFE, it was necessary to confirm the process of electron transfer occurred or not. Here, the electrochemical measurements were performed using a standard three-electrode system to estimate the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) energy levels of white-CPDs and hemin. The system included a saturated calomel (sat. KCl) electrode as reference electrode, a Pt wire electrode as counter electrode, and a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass with white-CPDs or hemin prepared on the surface as working electrode. Through the Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrical signals of white-CPDs and hemin were recorded with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH=7.4) as electrolyte, and their reduction potentials were determined to be -0.67 eV and -0.83 eV, respectively (Fig.S7A-B). While the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of white-CPDs and hemin could be depicted according to the following empirical formula [53,54].
(1)
E
HOMO
( e V )
= - e
(
φ
ox
+ 4.4 )
(2)
E
LUMO
( e V )
= - e
(
φ
red
+ 4.4 )

The φox and φred are onset of oxidation and reduction potential for white-CPDs/hemin, respectively. Therefore, the ELUMO was correspondingly calculated to be −3.73 eV and −3.57 eV. However, due to the irreversibility of the oxidation behavior (Fig.S7A-B), the EHOMO could not be obtained. So the EHOMO was estimated using the following equation:
(3)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO
-
E g

Where Eg means the band energy of white-CPDs and hemin, which could be estimated to be 3.96 and 2.92 eV from their Tauc plot, respectively (Fig.S7C-D, obtained according to the absorption edge from their UV-vis spectra).

Thus, the calculated EHOMO of white-CPDs and hemin was -7.69 and -6.49 eV. The differences between the estimated EHOMO or ELUMO of hemin and those reported previously might come from the differences in electrodes and parameters. As shown in Fig.9 , it was impossible for electron in the LUMO of white-CPDs transferred to the LUMO of hemin. Therefore, the PET could be excluded as well. Taken the above results together, the possible quenching mechanism could be attributed to IFE.

In addition, following experiments were designed to further investigate the possibility of the application on visualization detection. A fresh human blood sample (about 3 μL, collected from healthy volunteer from our laboratory) was dropped onto a filter paper which was cut into 1 cm square. Meanwhile, a piece of filter paper, with the same volume of coffee sample dropped on was prepared as a control. The two pieces of paper were put into white-CPDs solution and mixed respectively, and the digital photos were taken under ultraviolet light. Fig.10 showed that white-CPDs in the presence of paper with bloodstain exhibited a darker blue fluorescence under UV lamp, while the solution in the presence of paper with coffee still emitted white fluorescence almost as same as the original white-CPDs. These were consistent with the phenomenon shown in the inset of Fig. 6B, indicating that white-CPDs could be well applied to visualization detection of trace blood and its usability was further strengthened.

In this work, a new kind of biomass-CPDs from chlorophyll with tunable fluorescence colors was obtained by only controlled the conditions of hydrothermal reaction. The prepared CPDs exhibited red, white and blue emission under their optimal excitation with the CIE coordinates located at (0.52, 0.35), (0.34, 0.32) and (0.16, 0.19), respectively. Several kinds of characterizations identified mild carbonization of the CPDs as well as the occurrence of cross-linked polymerization process, and the fluorescence mechanism was speculated to be mainly influenced by CEE and surface states. Then a fluorescence system for hemin determination was put forward, which was based on the white-CPDs with multi-emission characteristic. The analysis results exhibited a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1-0.95 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.043 μM, and the quenching mechanism was considered to be caused by IFE. Furthermore, this fluorescence system has been successfully used for quantitative detection of hemin in serum as well as for visualization detection of blood sample. Therefore, this system was demonstrated as a simple, inexpensive sensing method with high sensitivity and accuracy, offered a new hemin nanoprobe for applications in disease diagnoses and trace identification, and also provided a way for the detection of other porphyrin-containing structural molecules.

Tianjiao Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Deming Dong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yingyi Meng: Resources, Investigation. Haijun Chen: Resources, Investigation. Chunyue Liu: Investigation. Zihan Qi: Validation. Anfeng Li: Resources. Yang Ning: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21404047).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123841.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
